sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 76, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ** Goal is to make all pink row symbols match top grid symbols
- Plan: ** Test by setting position 22 to its unique top-grid match fb80c9b281b063da, then check for feedback
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 75 actions; a strong score needs about 54 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
All 5 pink symbols now match top grid symbols, but the level is not done. So the goal is NOT simply to make all pink symbols match top grid symbols. I need to rethink.
Current pink: {15: b4edb3faf810133b, 22: fb80c9b281b063da, 29: b4edb3faf810133b, 36: f4469c244897540c, 43: cddd7d2c6a6ceea0}
All in top grid but level not done. So the goal is more specific than just "match top grid symbols".
Let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is to match specific symbols at specific positions. Let me look at the top grid layout more carefully:
Top grid pink symbols:
- (5, 23): cddd7d2c6a6ceea0
- (5, 46): d38ad0a046a963a0
- (14, 23): fb80c9b281b063da
- (14, 46): f4469c244897540c
- (23, 23): 12ee96cc93cd3e0a
- (23, 46): b4edb3faf810133b
Current pink row: b4edb3, fb80c9, b4edb3, f4469c, cddd7d
Hmm, maybe the goal is to match the pink row to a specific row of the top grid? Let me check:
- Top row pink: cddd7d, d38ad0 (but d38ad0 not in cycles)
- Middle row pink: fb80c9, f4469c
- Bottom row pink: 12ee96, b4edb3 (but 12ee96 not in cycles)
None of these have 5 symbols...
Wait, maybe the goal involves the sky blue row too. Let me look at the sky blue row symbols:
Sky blue row: {15: a5a03333dcefae48, 22: 598e9cbf8e60024f, 29: 9fd5f2ec9386bc10, 36: bb9796c6c98e1001, 43: 8e4cb671a12a4302}
Top grid sky blue symbols:
- (5, 13): 3f48311a76e5e733
- (5, 36): 88e45be75860ff8c
- (14, 13): 637b835795c0e7b2
- (14, 36): 04178689871e37b5
- (23, 13): 598e9cbf8e60024f
- (23, 36): 88e45be75860ff8c
Wait, 598e9cbf8e60024f appears in both the sky blue row (pos 22) and the top grid (23, 13). And 88e45be75860ff8c appears twice in the top grid.
Let me check: which sky blue row symbols appear in the top grid?
- a5a03333dcefae48: need to check
- 598e9cbf8e60024f: yes (23, 13)
- 9fd5f2ec9386bc10: need to check
- bb9796c6c98e1001: need to check
- 8e4cb671a12a4302: need to check
Actually, from my earlier analysis, only 1 sky blue row symbol (598e9cbf8e60024f at col 22) appeared in the top grid sky blue cells.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to make the pink row match the sky blue row in some way. Or maybe the goal is to make the pink row symbols all unique, or follow some other pattern.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to make the pink row match the sky blue row's symbols, but using the corresponding pink symbols from the top grid. For example, if the sky blue row has symbol X at position i, and the top grid has a pink symbol Y in the same row as X, then the pink row should have Y at position i.
But this is getting too speculative. Let me try a more systematic approach.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the top grid more carefully. The top grid has 3 rows and 4 columns. The columns alternate between sky blue and pink backgrounds:
Row 0: sky(5,13), pink(5,23), sky(5,36), pink(5,46)
Row 1: sky(14,13), pink(14,23), sky(14,36), pink(14,46)
Row 2: sky(23,13), pink(23,23), sky(23,36), pink(23,46)
The pink row has 5 positions. The top grid has 6 pink symbols (3 rows × 2 columns). Maybe the goal is to select 5 of the 6 pink symbols and arrange them in the pink row?
But which 5, and in what order? And some symbols don't appear in the cycles...
Let me try yet another hypothesis. What if the goal is to make the pink row match the top grid's pink symbols in reading order, skipping the ones that aren't available? The top grid pink symbols in reading order are:
cddd7d, d38ad0, fb80c9, f4469c, 12ee96, b4edb3
Available in cycles: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3 (4 symbols)
But we need 5 symbols...
Hmm, maybe the goal is different. Let me look at the current state again:
Pink: b4edb3, fb80c9, b4edb3, f4469c, cddd7d
Notice that b4edb3 appears twice (positions 15 and 29). Maybe the goal is to have all 5 symbols be unique? Or maybe there's a specific pattern.
Let me try to look at this from the perspective of the top grid's structure. The top grid has:
- 6 sky blue symbols (columns 0 and 2)
- 6 pink symbols (columns 1 and 3)
The lower section has:
- 5 sky blue symbols (fixed)
- 5 pink symbols (changeable)
Maybe the goal is to make the pink row correspond to the sky blue row in some way. For example, if the sky blue symbol at position i matches the top grid symbol at row r, column c, then the pink symbol at position i should be the top grid symbol at row r, column c+1 (or c-1).
Let me check this hypothesis. The sky blue row symbols are:
Pos 15: a5a03333dcefae48
Pos 22: 598e9cbf8e60024f
Pos 29: 9fd5f2ec9386bc10
Pos 36: bb9796c6c98e1001
Pos 43: 8e4cb671a12a4302
And I know 598e9cbf8e60024f is at (23, 13) in the top grid. The pink symbol in the same row is at (23, 23) which is 12ee96cc93cd3e0a. But 12ee96cc93cd3e0a doesn't appear in any cycle!
So this hypothesis doesn't work either.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to make the pink row match the sky blue row directly. But the sky blue symbols don't appear in the pink cycles...
Wait, maybe I'm wrong about that. Let me check if any sky blue row symbols appear in the pink cycles. I need to re-examine the cycle data.
Actually, from my earlier analysis:
- Pos 15 cycle has 3 top grid symbols: b4edb3, cddd7d, f4469c (all pink)
- Pos 22 cycle has 1 top grid symbol: fb80c9 (pink)
- Pos 29 cycle has 3 top grid symbols: b4edb3, cddd7d, f4469c (all pink)
- Pos 36 cycle has 3 top grid symbols: f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d (all pink)
- Pos 43 cycle has 3 top grid symbols: cddd7d, f4469c, b4edb3 (all pink)
So the top grid symbols that appear in cycles are all pink symbols. The sky blue row symbols don't appear in the pink cycles.
Let me try yet another hypothesis. What if the goal is to make the pink row match a specific permutation of the 4 available top-grid-pink symbols, with one repeated? The 4 available symbols are:
b4edb3, cddd7d, f4469c, fb80c9
And the current arrangement is: b4edb3, fb80c9, b4edb3, f4469c, cddd7d
Maybe the correct arrangement is different. Let me think about what pattern makes sense.
Looking at the top grid:
- Column 1 (pink): cddd7d, fb80c9, 12ee96 (12ee96 not available)
- Column 3 (pink): d38ad0, f4469c, b4edb3 (d38ad0 not available)
Available symbols by column:
- Col 1: cddd7d, fb80c9
- Col 3: f4469c, b4edb3
Maybe the goal is to alternate between col 1 and col 3 symbols? Like: cddd7d, f4469c, fb80c9, b4edb3, cddd7d? Or some other pattern?
Or maybe the goal is to match the top grid's rows. The top grid has 3 rows, and the pink row has 5 positions. Maybe the first 2 positions match row 0, the next 2 match row 1, and the last matches row 2?
Row 0 pink: cddd7d, d38ad0 (d38ad0 not available)
Row 1 pink: fb80c9, f4469c
Row 2 pink: 12ee96, b4edb3 (12ee96 not available)
This doesn't quite work either because some symbols aren't available.
I'm running out of ideas. Let me try a brute-force approach: try all combinations of the 4 available symbols and see which one completes the level. But that's 4^5 = 1024 combinations, which is too many.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The top grid has 6 pink symbols, but only 4 are available in the cycles. The 2 unavailable ones are d38ad0 (top-right) and 12ee96 (bottom-left). These are the "corner" symbols of the pink columns.
Maybe the goal is to use the 4 available symbols in a specific order. Let me look at the top grid layout again:
Row 0: sky, cddd7d, sky, d38ad0
Row 1: sky, fb80c9, sky, f4469c
Row 2: sky, 12ee96, sky, b4edb3
The 4 available symbols are: cddd7d (0,1), fb80c9 (1,1), f4469c (1,3), b4edb3 (2,3)
In reading order (row by row, left to right): cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3
Maybe the goal is to set the pink row to: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, ? (with one repeated)
Or maybe: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d (repeating the first)
Let me try this arrangement.
Current: b4edb3, fb80c9, b4edb3, f4469c, cddd7d
Target: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d
I need to change positions 15, 29, and 36.
Actually wait, maybe I should just try setting the pink row to match the top grid's available pink symbols in reading order, padded to 5 positions. Let me try: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d.
But this is just a guess. Let me try it and see.
Actually, I realize I should first check: what if the goal is simply to have all 5 symbols be different? Currently I have b4edb3 twice. Let me try making all 5 unique.
But there are only 4 available top-grid-pink symbols, so I can't have 5 unique ones from that set. Unless I use non-top-grid symbols too...
OK, I'm overthinking this. Let me just try a few specific arrangements and see what works. Let me start with the reading-order hypothesis: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
**Revised World Model:**
- All 5 pink symbols now match top grid symbols, but level NOT done
- Goal is more specific than just "match any top grid symbol"
- 4 available top-grid-pink symbols: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3
- Current: b4edb3, fb80c9, b4edb3, f4469c, cddd7d (b4edb3 repeated)
- **New hypothesis:** Goal is to arrange pink symbols in reading order of top grid: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d
- **Plan:** Set pink row to this arrangement and test

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current cursor is at pos 36 (col 36)
# Current pink: {15: b4edb3, 22: fb80c9, 29: b4edb3, 36: f4469c, 43: cddd7d}
# Target: {15: cddd7d, 22: fb80c9, 29: f4469c, 36: b4edb3, 43: cddd7d}
# Need to change: pos 15 (b4edb3->cddd7d), pos 29 (b4edb3->f4469c), pos 36 (f4469c->b4edb3)
# Pos 22 and 43 are already correct

# I need to figure out the cycle positions for each slot.
# Let me navigate to each position and cycle to the target.

seg = current_frame.segmentation

def get_pink_hash(seg, col):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'B':
            br = n['boundary']
            if br:
                min_r = min(p[0] for p in br)
                min_c = min(p[1] for p in br)
                if min_r == 52 and min_c == col:
                    return n['hash']
    return None

# Targets
targets = {15: 'cddd7d2c6a6ceea0', 22: 'fb80c9b281b063da', 29: 'f4469c244897540c', 36: 'b4edb3faf810133b', 43: 'cddd7d2c6a6ceea0'}

# I'm at pos 36. Let me go to pos 15 first (LEFT 3 times)
predict(expect_change=True, note="Go LEFT 3 to pos 15")
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])

# Now cycle to target at pos 15
seg = current_frame.segmentation
h = get_pink_hash(seg, 15)
target = targets[15]
print(f"Pos 15: current={h[:8]}..., target={target[:8]}...")

for i in range(7):
    if h == target:
        print(f"  Match at pos 15 after {i} UPs")
        break
    predict(expect_change=True)
    action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    h = get_pink_hash(seg, 15)

# Go to pos 29 (RIGHT 2)
predict(expect_change=True, note="Go RIGHT 2 to pos 29")
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
h = get_pink_hash(seg, 29)
target = targets[29]
print(f"Pos 29: current={h[:8]}..., target={target[:8]}...")

for i in range(7):
    if h == target:
        print(f"  Match at pos 29 after {i} UPs")
        break
    predict(expect_change=True)
    action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    h = get_pink_hash(seg, 29)

# Go to pos 36 (RIGHT 1)
predict(expect_change=True, note="Go RIGHT 1 to pos 36")
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
h = get_pink_hash(seg, 36)
target = targets[36]
print(f"Pos 36: current={h[:8]}..., target={target[:8]}...")

for i in range(7):
    if h == target:
        print(f"  Match at pos 36 after {i} UPs")
        break
    predict(expect_change=True)
    action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    h = get_pink_hash(seg, 36)

# Check final state
current_pink = {}
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B':
        br = n['boundary']
        if br:
            min_r = min(p[0] for p in br)
            min_c = min(p[1] for p in br)
            if min_r == 52:
                current_pink[min_c] = n['hash']

print("\nFinal pink:", {k: v[:8] for k, v in current_pink.items()})
print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done', False))
print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed', False))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  Match at pos 29 after 4 UPs
Pos 36: current=f4469c24..., target=b4edb3fa...
  Match at pos 36 after 3 UPs

Final pink: {15: 'cddd7d2c', 22: 'fb80c9b2', 29: 'f4469c24', 36: 'b4edb3fa', 43: 'cddd7d2c'}
Done: False
Level completed: False

Maybe the goal is to make the pink row correspond to the sky blue row in some way. For example, if the sky blue symbol at position i matches the top grid symbol at row r, column c, then the pink symbol at position i should be the top grid symbol at row r, column c+1 (or c-1).
Let me check this hypothesis. The sky blue row symbols are:
Pos 15: a5a03333dcefae48
Pos 22: 598e9cbf8e60024f
Pos 29: 9fd5f2ec9386bc10
Pos 36: bb9796c6c98e1001
Pos 43: 8e4cb671a12a4302
And I know 598e9cbf8e60024f is at (23, 13) in the top grid. The pink symbol in the same row is at (23, 23) which is 12ee96cc93cd3e0a. But 12ee96cc93cd3e0a doesn't appear in any cycle!
So this hypothesis doesn't work either.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to make the pink row match the sky blue row directly. But the sky blue symbols don't appear in the pink cycles...
Wait, maybe I'm wrong about that. Let me check if any sky blue row symbols appear in the pink cycles. I need to re-examine the cycle data.
Actually, from my earlier analysis:
- Pos 15 cycle has 3 top grid symbols: b4edb3, cddd7d, f4469c (all pink)
- Pos 22 cycle has 1 top grid symbol: fb80c9 (pink)
- Pos 29 cycle has 3 top grid symbols: b4edb3, cddd7d, f4469c (all pink)
- Pos 36 cycle has 3 top grid symbols: f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d (all pink)
- Pos 43 cycle has 3 top grid symbols: cddd7d, f4469c, b4edb3 (all pink)
So the top grid symbols that appear in cycles are all pink symbols. The sky blue row symbols don't appear in the pink cycles.
Let me try yet another hypothesis. What if the goal is to make the pink row match a specific permutation of the 4 available top-grid-pink symbols, with one repeated? The 4 available symbols are:
b4edb3, cddd7d, f4469c, fb80c9
And the current arrangement is: b4edb3, fb80c9, b4edb3, f4469c, cddd7d
Maybe the correct arrangement is different. Let me think about what pattern makes sense.
Looking at the top grid:
- Column 1 (pink): cddd7d, fb80c9, 12ee96 (12ee96 not available)
- Column 3 (pink): d38ad0, f4469c, b4edb3 (d38ad0 not available)
Available symbols by column:
- Col 1: cddd7d, fb80c9
- Col 3: f4469c, b4edb3
Maybe the goal is to alternate between col 1 and col 3 symbols? Like: cddd7d, f4469c, fb80c9, b4edb3, cddd7d? Or some other pattern?
Or maybe the goal is to match the top grid's rows. The top grid has 3 rows, and the pink row has 5 positions. Maybe the first 2 positions match row 0, the next 2 match row 1, and the last matches row 2?
Row 0 pink: cddd7d, d38ad0 (d38ad0 not available)
Row 1 pink: fb80c9, f4469c

This doesn't quite work either because some symbols aren't available.
I'm running out of ideas. Let me try a brute-force approach: try all combinations of the 4 available symbols and see which one completes the level. But that's 4^5 = 1024 combinations, which is too many.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The top grid has 6 pink symbols, but only 4 are available in the cycles. The 2 unavailable ones are d38ad0 (top-right) and 12ee96 (bottom-left). These are the "corner" symbols of the pink columns.
Maybe the goal is to use the 4 available symbols in a specific order. Let me look at the top grid layout again:

The 4 available symbols are: cddd7d (0,1), fb80c9 (1,1), f4469c (1,3), b4edb3 (2,3)
In reading order (row by row, left to right): cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3
Maybe the goal is to set the pink row to: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, ? (with one repeated)
Or maybe: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d (repeating the first)

Target: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d
I need to change positions 15, 29, and 36.
Actually wait, maybe I should just try setting the pink row to match the top grid's available pink symbols in reading order, padded to 5 positions. Let me try: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d.
But this is just a guess. Let me try it and see.
Actually, I realize I should first check: what if the goal is simply to have all 5 symbols be different? Currently I have b4edb3 twice. Let me try making all 5 unique.
But there are only 4 available top-grid-pink symbols, so I can't have 5 unique ones from that set. Unless I use non-top-grid symbols too...
OK, I'm overthinking this. Let me just try a few specific arrangements and see what works. Let me start with the reading-order hypothesis: cddd7d, fb80c9, f4469c, b4edb3, cddd7d.

sections.content:

- All 5 pink symbols now match top grid symbols, but level NOT done

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current cursor is at pos 36 (col 36)
# Current pink: {15: b4edb3, 22: fb80c9, 29: b4edb3, 36: f4469c, 43: cddd7d}
# Target: {15: cddd7d, 22: fb80c9, 29: f4469c, 36: b4edb3, 43: cddd7d}
# Need to change: pos 15 (b4edb3->cddd7d), pos 29 (b4edb3->f4469c), pos 36 (f4469c->b4edb3)
# Pos 22 and 43 are already correct

# I need to figure out the cycle positions for each slot.
# Let me navigate to each position and cycle to the target.

seg = current_frame.segmentation

def get_pink_hash(seg, col):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'B':
            br = n['boundary']
            if br:
                min_r = min(p[0] for p in br)
                min_c = min(p[1] for p in br)
                if min_r == 52 and min_c == col:
                    return n['hash']
    return None

# Targets
targets = {15: 'cddd7d2c6a6ceea0', 22: 'fb80c9b281b063da', 29: 'f4469c244897540c', 36: 'b4edb3faf810133b', 43: 'cddd7d2c6a6ceea0'}

# I'm at pos 36. Let me go to pos 15 first (LEFT 3 times)
predict(expect_change=True, note="Go LEFT 3 to pos 15")
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])

# Now cycle to target at pos 15
seg = current_frame.segmentation
h = get_pink_hash(seg, 15)
target = targets[15]
print(f"Pos 15: current={h[:8]}..., target={target[:8]}...")

for i in range(7):
    if h == target:
        print(f"  Match at pos 15 after {i} UPs")
        break
    predict(expect_change=True)
    action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    h = get_pink_hash(seg, 15)

# Go to pos 29 (RIGHT 2)
predict(expect_change=True, note="Go RIGHT 2 to pos 29")
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
h = get_pink_hash(seg, 29)
target = targets[29]
print(f"Pos 29: current={h[:8]}..., target={target[:8]}...")

for i in range(7):
    if h == target:
        print(f"  Match at pos 29 after {i} UPs")
        break
    predict(expect_change=True)
    action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    h = get_pink_hash(seg, 29)

# Go to pos 36 (RIGHT 1)
predict(expect_change=True, note="Go RIGHT 1 to pos 36")
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
h = get_pink_hash(seg, 36)
target = targets[36]
print(f"Pos 36: current={h[:8]}..., target={target[:8]}...")

for i in range(7):
    if h == target:
        print(f"  Match at pos 36 after {i} UPs")
        break
    predict(expect_change=True)
    action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    h = get_pink_hash(seg, 36)

# Check final state
current_pink = {}
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B':
        br = n['boundary']
        if br:
            min_r = min(p[0] for p in br)
            min_c = min(p[1] for p in br)
            if min_r == 52:
                current_pink[min_c] = n['hash']

print("\nFinal pink:", {k: v[:8] for k, v in current_pink.items()})
print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done', False))
print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed', False))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
